**Culantro** (also known as **Bhandhanya**, **Chandon benit**, **Culantro**, **Culantro Coyote**, **Fitweed**, **Long coriander**, **Mexican coriander**, **Wild coriander**, **Recao**, **Spiritweed**, **Ngò gai**, **Sawtooth**, and **Saw-leaf herb**), is the leaves of the herb ***Eryngium foetidum***.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) It is native to Mexico and South America, but is cultivated worldwide.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1) In the United States, where it is not well-known,[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) the name culantro sometimes causes confusion with *Coriandrum sativum*, the leaves of which are known as *cilantro*.

## Characteristics

[edit | edit source]

Culantro leaves are long and green, with, serrated edges.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) The aroma and flavor are comparable to those of cilantro,[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) though culantro is stronger.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3)

## Selection and storage

[edit | edit source]

Like other leafy greens, culantro should be stored in the fridge, wrapped in paper towels inside an airtight container. They will keep like this for a week or so.[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2)

## Use

[edit | edit source]

Culantro is widely used in seasoning and marinating in Latin America and the Caribbean.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) It is also used extensively in Thailand, India, Vietnam, and other parts of Asia as a culinary herb, much like cilantro is.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)[[3]](#cite_note-:2-3) This variety of coriander dries well, retaining good color and flavor, making it valuable in the dried herb industry.

Culantro is sometimes used as a substitute for cilantro, but has a much stronger taste. Another difference is that culantro can be included during cooking,[[2]](#cite_note-:1-2) since it is fairly robust.[[1]](#cite_note-:0-1)
